quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Observational study"?

options.text: The initial probability of an event before new evidence is considered; the starting point in Bayesian reasoning
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)
A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable

quiz_questions.explanation: "Observational study" is defined as: A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Confounding variable"?

options.text: Neither the subjects nor the researchers measuring outcomes know which treatment each subject received, minimizing both placebo effect and experimenter bias
μ = np; the expected number of successes in n trials
A measure of the strength and direction of the linear relationship between two quantitative variables; ranges from -1 to +1
A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them

quiz_questions.explanation: "Confounding variable" is defined as: A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Lurking variable"?

options.text: The square root of variance; measures the typical distance of observations from the mean in the original units of measurement
A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied
t = (x̄ - μ₀)/(s/√n); replaces the z-statistic when σ is unknown, using sample standard deviation s in the denominator
An unobserved variable that influences both the explanatory and response variables, creating a false impression of a direct relationship

quiz_questions.explanation: "Lurking variable" is defined as: A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Causation vs. association"?

options.text: As the number of trials increases, the sample proportion of successes converges toward the true probability
Minimum, Q1, median, Q3, and maximum—the basis for constructing boxplots and identifying outliers
The claim we suspect is true and are trying to find evidence for; can be one-sided () or two-sided (≠)
Association means two variables are related; causation requires that changing one variable directly produces a change in the other

quiz_questions.explanation: "Causation vs. association" is defined as: Association means two variables are related; causation requires that changing one variable directly produces a change in the other. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Retrospective vs. prospective study"?

options.text: Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable
μ = 1/p; the expected number of trials needed to achieve the first success
Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
A branching diagram showing sequential probabilities along paths; multiplying along a path gives joint probability, adding across paths gives marginal probability

quiz_questions.explanation: "Retrospective vs. prospective study" is defined as: Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects"?

options.text: Standardized residual
Heavy tails
Observational study
Prior probability

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Observational study".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A variable associated with both the explanatory variable and the response variable that distorts the apparent relationship between them"?

options.text: Establishing causation
Cramér's V
Margin of error (for means)
Confounding variable

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Confounding variable".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A variable not included in the study that may influence the relationship between the variables being studied"?

options.text: Lurking variable
Cumulative distribution function (CDF)
Cramér's V
CLT for proportions

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Lurking variable".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Association means two variables are related; causation requires that changing one variable directly produces a change in the other"?

options.text: Causation vs. association
Median
Standard normal distribution
LINE conditions

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Causation vs. association".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start"?

options.text: Required sample size
Replication
Retrospective vs. prospective study
Coefficient of determination (r²)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Retrospective vs. prospective study".

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the definition of 'Observational study'?

options.text: A study in which the researcher observes and records data without imposing a treatment or intervention on the subjects
A study in which the researcher deliberately imposes one or more treatments on subjects to observe the effect on a response variable
Tests H₀: μ₁ = μ₂ (or μ₁ - μ₂ = 0) using t = (x̄₁ - x̄₂)/√(s₁²/n₁ + s₂²/n₂)

options.text: Retrospective studies look backward at existing data; prospective studies follow subjects forward in time from the start
A branching diagram showing sequential probabilities along paths; multiplying along a path gives joint probability, adding across paths gives marginal probability
Randomly selecting entire groups (clusters) and surveying all individuals within chosen clusters; practical when a population list is unavailable

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Observational Studies vs Experiments is accurate?

options.text: Interpretation is crucial: a 95% CI means that 95% of all intervals constructed this way capture the true p.
Observational Studies vs Experiments differ fundamentally in whether the researcher imposes a treatment.
Sampling Distribution of Sample Proportions describes the distribution of p̂ = X/n across all possible samples, where X is the count of successes in n independent trials.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Observational Studies vs Experiments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Observational studies simply observe and record data without intervening, making them unable to establish causation due to lurking and confounding variables.
Two-way tables (contingency tables) organize counts for two categorical variables and allow calculation of marginal, joint, and conditional distributions.
Sampling Distribution of Sample Means describes the probability distribution of the sample mean x̄ across all possible samples of size n from a population.
Conditional probability is fundamental to updating beliefs with new information and is the basis of Bayes' Theorem, which reverses conditional probabilities.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Observational Studies vs Experiments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Data Collection and Types of Variables forms the foundation of statistical analysis by distinguishing between categorical variables (which assign labels or categories) and quantitative variables (which take numerical measurements).
Experiments use random assignment to eliminate confounding, allowing causal conclusions.

The four-step process involves stating hypotheses, checking conditions (random sample, 10% condition, np₀ ≥ 10 and n(1-p₀) ≥ 10), calculating the test statistic and p-value, and stating a conclusion in context.
Residual plots—graphs of residuals versus x—are essential diagnostic tools: a random scatter indicates the linear model is appropriate, while patterns suggest non-linearity.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Observational Studies vs Experiments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: The addition rule for mutually exclusive events (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B)) and the general addition rule (P(A or B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A and B)) handle unions, while the multiplication rule handles intersections.
The CLT also applies to sample proportions and sums.
The geometric distribution counts the number of trials until the first success: P(X=k) = (1-p)^(k-1)·p, with mean 1/p.
Understanding this distinction is critical—many important questions (smoking's effect on health, educational interventions) rely on observational data when experiments are unethical or impractical.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Observational Studies vs Experiments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Comparing proportions is essential in clinical trials, A/B testing, and any study comparing success rates between groups.

The binomial distribution counts the number of successes in a fixed number of independent trials with constant success probability: P(X=k) = C(n,k)p^k(1-p)^(n-k), with mean np and standard deviation √(np(1-p)).
Observational Studies vs Experiments differ fundamentally in whether the researcher imposes a treatment.
Model comparison using r² and residual plots helps determine which model (linear, exponential, power) best fits the data.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Observational Studies vs Experiments as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Election polling using CLT to justify normal-based margin of error calculations even though individual voter responses (yes/no) are binary, not normal
Economists checking LINE conditions before publishing a regression model of how education level predicts income
The classic ice cream and drowning correlation: both increase in summer due to the lurking variable of temperature, not because ice cream causes drowning
Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Reading Minitab/R/Excel regression output to identify the slope estimate, standard error, and confidence interval for a research report
Identifying confounding variables in public health studies, such as separating the effect of diet from exercise on heart disease rates
Modeling customer arrival times at a store using appropriate probability distributions to optimize staffing schedules
Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Observational Studies vs Experiments applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: A nutritionist testing whether the mean sodium content of a product exceeds 500 mg using a sample of 15 packages and a one-sample t-test
Epidemiological prospective cohort studies (like the Framingham Heart Study) tracking thousands of people over decades to identify cardiovascular risk factors
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other
Environmental agency testing whether mean mercury concentration in fish exceeds the 0.5 ppm safety limit (H₀: μ = 0.5, Hₐ: μ > 0.5) to issue consumption advisories

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Observational Studies vs Experiments?

options.text: Tobacco litigation using decades of observational evidence (cohort studies, dose-response relationships) to establish smoking as a cause of lung cancer when experiments were unethical
The classic ice cream and drowning correlation: both increase in summer due to the lurking variable of temperature, not because ice cream causes drowning
Educational researchers using a one-sample t-test to determine whether average ACT scores in a district differ from the national mean of 21
Education policy comparing the distribution of reading proficiency levels (below, at, above grade level) across different school districts

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Observational Studies vs Experiments in practice?

options.text: Evaluating whether a new teaching method causes improved test scores requires an experiment with random assignment—simply comparing schools using different methods is observational
Taste tests where each consumer rates two products, analyzing the paired differences to determine if there is a preference for one product over the other
Sociological research examining whether political party affiliation is independent of geographic region using survey data from a national random sample
Manufacturing tolerance analysis using variance to ensure parts fall within acceptable dimensional limits

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Observational Studies vs Experiments.
